input.query: memory notes forgetting recall retention grounding evidence citations evidence linked verifiable claims claim checking planning decomposition lightweight planning replan

results.title: CoverageTrap: Why Greedy Paper Selection Misses Evidence Linking
PlanSketch: Lightweight Decomposition for Small Review Tasks
Stale Reflection Notes and the Cost of Unchecked Recall
MemoryPolicy: When Notes Help and When They Mislead

output.title: CoverageTrap: Why Greedy Paper Selection Misses Evidence Linking
output.abstract: Greedy top-k selection misses papers about evidence linking and verifiable claims when relevant work uses different surface terms.
findings: Coverage-aware selection and topic-specific replanning recover missing evidence better than top-k greedy selection.

output.title: PlanSketch: Lightweight Decomposition for Small Review Tasks
output.abstract: Small literature reviews benefit from lightweight decomposition and selective replanning instead of exhaustive search trees.
findings: Lightweight decomposition adds enough structure for small review tasks without heavy formal planning.

output.title: Stale Reflection Notes and the Cost of Unchecked Recall
output.abstract: Legacy notes tend to overstate reflection benefits unless they are revalidated against current local evidence.
findings: Unchecked stale memory makes reflection look more reliable than the evidence supports.

output.title: MemoryPolicy: When Notes Help and When They Mislead
output.abstract: Stored notes improve continuity only when retrieval and forgetting policies are explicit. Stale notes create confident but brittle synthesis.
findings: Memory must include retrieval and forgetting policy, not just storage.
